event_summary: Iran announced a new body to manage the Strait of Hormuz on 18 May 2026 as Trump warned Tehran the clock was ticking on a deal; the Trump-Xi Beijing summit produced warm exchanges but no Iran breakthrough, and global companies reported $25 billion in war-related costs.
tldr: The headline layer distributes the Hormuz story across four co-present frames with no single dominant: ECONOMIC (energy cost pass-through and global corporate bill, 14 buckets), SECURITY_DEFENSE (military escalation signals and territorial control, 14 buckets), POLITICAL (Trump deal pressure and summit diplomacy, 11 buckets), and EXTERNAL_REGULATION (Iran's unilateral Hormuz management body, sanctions, yuan invoicing, 7 buckets). The $25 billion corporate-cost headline, carried identically by Japan, Pakistan, and pan_Arab, is the most striking cross-bloc convergence in the corpus. Syria's Syrian-pound headline — the only title with no Hormuz frame — is the sharpest headline-layer silence. Germany's Spiegel carries the most alarmist framing: 'Iran Must Only Succeed Once to Trigger a Catastrophe.'

isolation_top.note: Reuters 'opaque oil deals and petrodollar decline' headline frames a structural financial-system story that diverges semantically from the military-or-political dominant.
Al Jazeera on-the-ground Hormuz Island report ('Iran says it controls the strait') emphasises territorial sovereignty — semantically distinct from the deal-or-escalate framing majority.
BBC Africa's Kenya transport-strike headline frames the crisis through downstream African labour disruption — semantically distant from Middle East military and diplomatic coverage.
CGTN/UKMTO headlines use a multilateral-interest register ('not in common interests', 'maritime access restrictions') — Chinese diplomatic framing distinct from the Trump-pressure dominant.
Syrian Observer headline about Syrian pound and the central bank governor carries no Hormuz frame — a domestic-finance story likely captured by regional proximity rather than topical relevance.

silences.what_they_covered_instead: Syrian Observer headlined a domestic Syrian finance story (exchange rate and central bank governor) with no visible Hormuz or Iran connection at the title level.

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only headline in the corpus to frame Hormuz risk through a named military expert using catastrophic-risk language — 'Iran Must Only Succeed Once' — the most alarmist SECURITY_DEFENSE framing in the 52-article corpus.

Only headline to frame Hormuz as a structural marker of US imperial decline ('Like Suez for the Brits, Hormuz spells doom for US empire') — an EXTERNAL_REGULATION / historical-analogy frame absent from every other bucket.

bottom_line: Headlines across the 30-bucket Hormuz corpus distribute into four co-present frames — ECONOMIC cost pass-through, SECURITY_DEFENSE escalation, POLITICAL deal pressure, and EXTERNAL_REGULATION (Iran's new Hormuz management body) — a wider frame spread than most single-story corpora and consistent with a crisis that is simultaneously a military standoff, an energy-price event, a diplomatic negotiation, and a sovereignty dispute. Syria's central-bank headline is the clearest title-layer silence; Germany's Spiegel sets the high-alarm outlier.
